item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the log a lot, but the description itself was a 'meh' read. I actually don't like this scip because it's one of those touch-it-and-it's-game-over objects. Other than the creativeness of what happens to a victim, these are no more interesting than stepping on a land mine.

In this case, I really have a problem with it because of how stubbornly written it is in terms of how inescapable the contest is (See Addendum 1212-A).

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Given that SCP-3311 apparently has infinite chairs, and has created replicas of other SCP chairs before, its likely the SCPF would keep an eye out for more instances of 1212 that may appear there. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1212 is kept in a 5m x 5m x 3m containment cell at Site-██. Experimentation may only be performed with prior permission from at least two (2) Level 3 personnel, and the room is to be cleared of any residual body fluids or other detritus immediately after testing.

To this:

SCP-1212 is kept in a 5m x 5m x 3m containment cell at Site-██. Experimentation may only be performed with prior permission from at least two (2) Level 3 personnel, and the room is to be cleared of any residual body fluids or other detritus immediately after testing.

Any instances of SCP-1212 discovered within SCP-3311 are to be immediately be moved to Site-██ for long term storage.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can we get a story about the at least one guy who drank this thing under the table and stayed conscious? Dude must have an iron liver.

Or at least think he does.

Holy shit, it could totally just be a "how good a drinker do you really think you are" test, couldn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
scary idea (I always have to think of the real cases of deadly drinking games when I read that), greatly executed, heavy upvote.
But there's still one very minor thing: First it says "There is always a period of between thirty (30) and fifty (50) seconds between each sequence" and later "an average of one (1) drink per minute". This should be slightly adjusted, because the drinking process itself is described as "one smooth motion". That won't take 20 seconds, would it? Maybe set the period between each sequence to between fourty (40) and sixty (60) seconds or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On one hand, it's like the coffee cup. On the other hand, it's like an extreme, slightly less disgusting but probably more awful version of the coffee cup.

Take your +1, you sick man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm an alcoholic and this terrifies me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slowly, he keels over and falls off the stool, landing in a now large puddle of bodily fluid.

Slowly, he keels over and falls off the stool,

Slowly, he keels over

he keels over

keels over

I don't really think this needs explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PMFJI, but if the drinks are in a shot glass or a tumbler that's wide (and strong) enough for the contestant to pick up by gripping the rim in their teeth, they don't need arms (of any kind) to knock em back.

I miss college…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Original, well written, and good addendum experiment thingies. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for general goodness.
Upvoted *at* the line "oral expulsion of liquids". So clinical it was hysterical for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well considering the fact that there's no signs of actual alcohol in their system at the time of death or release, save for their actual SYMPTOMS, don't think that would help.

Of course, if Keith were to sit on the chair, he'd probably describe it as a "fairly mellow Friday night."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an interesting thought. I'd have to say that the contest would still continue, and the drinking would happen as if the contestant still had their arms. They would hallucinate that they have arms during the contest. It's a full-body possession that basically ignores the physical reality of the subject's condition while in the chair, which is what I tried to get across with the first addendum. It doesn't really put alcohol in the bloodstream, it just convinces the body that it has, and it'll just as soon convince an armless man he has arms if it means he can participate in the contest, if that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had an interesting idea for a test.

Test cheating.

That is, pull a Keith Richards while the test is underway: keep swapping out their blood with 'new' blood. =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Very nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks good to me now. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The very last spoken line in the second addendum is pretty awkward. Other than that, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it when they were drinking three drinks a minute for four hours. If the SCP can make them ignore their frigging arms being cut off, it can make them do whatever else it wants. The alcohol isn't real, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that this was posted on a Friday night. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good concept, great interview. Upvoted. Side note: that looks exactly like the stool I'm using as a bedside table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what happens if someone who's already lost their arms sits in the chair? Does it fail to activate, does the subject hallucinate that their arms are back, or do their arms physically come back for the duration of the contest?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion, using "rude" in that manner gives a non-clinical tone to the document that the Foundation wouldn't use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote for now. I gave you my comments in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article itself is more fascinating than amazing, but that test log made me react with a breathy "Wow." Very nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test log still says he's in there for over three hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback - I've taken it on board and changed both the duration of the test and the frequency of drinking, after looking at some estimates about alcohol poisoning in more detail. I've also added a phrase that removes any ambiguity about the physical presence of alcohol in the test subjects, and edited the log narrator dialogue to sound more clinical. Thanks for pointing out the bit about the volume, I clearly didn't think hard enough about just how much liquid the thing was making them drink.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Scantron. If these are hallucinatory drinks, then maybe a line with an autopsy of a D-class showing there was no actual alcohol in their bloodstream. If there really is alcohol, then they can really hold their liquor! At four hours with one ounce shots they would have taken in over 20 liters of fluid, which is biologically impossible to survive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These must be really weak drinks, then; or the contestant is supernaturally hardened against the effects of drinking. Neither of which are stated or noted as a possibility. As-is, it breaks suspension of disbelief for me.

EDIT: I like this SCP, to be clear. It's just that this bit is bugging me enough to make me not want to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The drinks are hallucinations brought on by the scp; who knows how many they can drink?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
of a crude construction or kind

Just my opinion but I think it'd be simpler if it was simply crude rather than rude, though both are acceptable for the situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A period of time elapses that is no less than four (4) hours in length, with an average of (3) 'drinks' per minute

4h * 60min/h * 3 drinks/min = 720 drinks. And yet somehow ten or more people managed to survive that. This is really, really unrealistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh Lord, that test log is fantastic. And horrible. It's horribly fantastic. It'd be cool to toy around with the idea of the appearance of the stool/hallucination being linked to the personality/mental state of the contestant, but the entry is great as it is.

Needless to say, upvoted the hell out of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
rude
… 
5. roughly wrought, built, or formed; of a crude construction or kind: a rude cottage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea. Drinking game from hell! My only advice would be to clear up some of your word choices and make it a little bit more clinical in certain areas. Still, I like it a lot!
And:

a rude wooden stool which appeared to have been painted very poorly with black paint

Do you mean "crude?" :3
